default_annotator.report_text:
the group still deploys lightweight JavaScript backdoor with communication over HTTPS mimicking Content Delivery Network (CDN) domains The backdoor uses HTTPS for communication, which is an application layer protocol. Mimicking CDN domains indicates that the traffic is designed to blend in with legitimate web traffic.

they still leverage JavaScript backdoor via renamedwscript.exe with the actual JavaScript code called "errors.txt," for example The backdoor is implemented using JavaScript, executed via the Windows Script Host (wscript.exe), which is a common scripting interpreter.

the macro logic copies the original JavaScript execution enginewscript.exe in %LOCALAPPDATA% wscript.exe is used to execute JavaScript scripts. The adversaries rely on this interpreter to execute their malicious JavaScript.

checking the system drive size viaGetDrive.TotalSize of more than 2456 bytes to possibly thwart anti-sandbox check The malware performs checks on the system environment to detect if it is running in a sandbox (e.g., by checking the size of the drive).

The FIN7 Microsoft document loaders do not rely on any exploits but simply require a social engineering trick (https://www.sentinelone.com/blog/phishing-revealingvulnerable-targets/) to "Enable Content" to activate macros. a Microsoft document is used.

The final execution of the backdoor is performed via this following command: %LOCALAPPDATA%mses.exe //b /e:jscript %temp%errors.txt The command executes a JavaScript backdoor using mses.exe, which is associated with executing JScript.

The actual obfuscated Javascript backdoor is stored in UserForm object, which is also written to a disc as "errors.txt" in%TEMP% an obfuscated JavaScript backdoor is used

The malware checks for the presence of virtual machine checking whether the malware is running in a virtualized environment

queries active directory This refers to querying Active Directory to discover domain trust relationships, which helps attackers gather information about the environment.

operating system, screen resolution Retrieving information about the operating system and the screen resolution

retrieves a process list. Retrieving a list of running processes helps attackers understand the environment, identify security software, or locate valuable processes to exploit.
